name: Kkakdugi
subtitle: Crunchy cubed radish kimchi
blrNote: Indian White Radish (Mooli) is a perfect substitute for Korean Mu. It's a bit more pungent but holds its crunch beautifully. Kashmiri Mirch gives the right colour without excessive heat.

ingredients.name: White Radish
substitutions: Korean Mu (if available at Korean grocery)

ingredients.name: Sugar
ingredients.notes: Feeds the bacteria and balances the heat

equipment.name: Disposable gloves
equipment.notes: The chilli stains and burns skin

tldr: Salt radish cubes until they weep, toss with chilli and garlic, pack tight. Maximum crunch.

steps.title: Cube & Sweat
steps.instruction: Cube radish into 1-inch squares. Toss with salt and sugar. Let sit for 30–60 minutes until a pool of water forms at the bottom of the bowl.
tips: Don't skip the sweating phase - the drawn-out water becomes the fermentation liquid
If using Indian mooli instead of Korean mu, peel the skin - mooli skin can turn rubbery during fermentation.
steps.checkpoint: Visible pool of liquid at the bottom, cubes are slightly wilted

steps.title: Season
steps.instruction: Do NOT rinse! Add chilli powder, minced garlic, grated ginger, and fish sauce if using. Mix until every single cube is coated red.
tips: Wear gloves - the chilli will burn your fingers for hours
Taste a cube: it should be salty, spicy, and slightly sweet
If using Kashmiri mirch, you may want to add a small pinch of regular red chilli for heat - Kashmiri mirch is mostly colour with mild spice.
steps.checkpoint: Uniform red coating on every cube

steps.title: Pack
steps.instruction: Pack into a jar, pressing down firmly to remove air pockets. The radish liquid should rise to cover the cubes. Leave 3cm headspace.
tips: Use a clean fist or a wooden spoon to push down
If there isn't enough liquid, add a splash of 2% brine
Place a piece of cling wrap directly on the surface before sealing the jar - it reduces air contact and prevents kahm yeast.
steps.checkpoint: Cubes submerged under their own liquid

steps.title: Room Temp
steps.instruction: Leave out at room temperature for 1–2 days until you see bubbles forming, then move to the fridge. In BLR heat, this can happen in 24 hours.
tips: Taste daily - when it's tangy enough for you, refrigerate
In the fridge, it continues to develop flavour for weeks
Burp the jar once a day during room-temperature fermentation - kkakdugi produces a lot of CO2 and can overflow if sealed tight.

culturalContext.story: Kkakdugi is the inseparable companion to Seolleongtang (설렁탕 - ox bone soup). The crunchy, spicy cubes cut through the rich, milky broth perfectly. Every Korean grandmother has her own recipe, and these are often the first kimchi a child learns to make.
culturalContext.historicalNote: Legend attributes its creation to Princess Sukseon of the Joseon Dynasty, though fermented radish preparations exist across Korean food history long before that. During kimjang (kimchi-making season), kkakdugi was always made alongside the larger baechu kimchi batches.
culturalContext.significance: While baechu (napa cabbage) kimchi gets all the international fame, kkakdugi is the everyday workhorse - faster to make, more forgiving, and crunchy in a way that cabbage simply can't match.
culturalContext.funFact: The name "깍두기" is an onomatopoeia - it mimics the sound of a knife cutting through a crisp radish. Korean food naming is surprisingly musical.

thingsToAccountFor.title: Don't rinse after sweating
thingsToAccountFor.description: The salty liquid drawn from the radish IS your fermentation brine. Rinsing it away means you'll need to add external brine and lose flavour.

thingsToAccountFor.title: BLR heat = fast ferment
thingsToAccountFor.description: At 25°C+, kkakdugi can over-sour in just 2 days. Check daily and refrigerate as soon as it's tangy enough.

variations.name: Vegan Kkakdugi
variations.description: Drop the fish sauce, add 1 tbsp soy sauce + 1 tsp miso paste for umami.

variations.name: Apple Kkakdugi
variations.description: Add diced Korean pear or apple to the seasoning for natural sweetness.
